video_title: Colin Lancaster, Co-Head of Discretionary Macro and Fixed Income at Schoenfeld Strategic Advisors
gist: Colin Lancaster of Schoenfeld argues markets are looking through a persistent energy/crude shock and structurally sticky 3.5% inflation toward enormous S&P earnings, while a woefully asymmetric Fed eases into above-target inflation. He details how multi-manager pod shops manufacture uncorrelated alpha, why crowded levered positions (including 3x single-stock ETFs and the AI/memory theme) are the key tail risk, and why a 'skinny non-deal' on Iran keeps crude elevated into the midterms.

insights.claim: The Fed is woefully asymmetric: it bought bonds in 2015 with inflation at 1.5% yet is discussing easing in 2025 with inflation at 3.5%, undermining the credibility of its 2% target.
insights.speaker: Colin Lancaster
insights.so_what: An easing cycle into above-target inflation argues for structurally higher term premium and steeper curves; fade the durability of the 2% anchor in rates pricing.
insights.quote: We were buying bonds in 2015 with inflation at 1.5%. And now we're talking about easing in 2025 with inflation at 3.5%. It just doesn't make sense.

insights.claim: The US price-level problem cannot be solved by monetary policy alone because the 2020-2021 fiscal 'airdrop of dollars' is structurally baked in, and no politician can win on a belt-tightening platform.
insights.speaker: Colin Lancaster
insights.so_what: Fiscal dominance implies inflation stays sticky regardless of Fed action; favors real assets and inflation-hedged positioning over duration.
insights.quote: You can't win an election with a belt tightening uh platform. That's just not going to work.

insights.claim: Inflation has run above the Fed's target for roughly 60 months and will be well more than that before it dips below 2%, creating a 'quiet vigilanteism' where the market simply stops believing the 2% target.
insights.speaker: Dean Curnutt
insights.so_what: A gradual loss of target credibility (vs a sharp Liz Trust event) erodes long-bond value over time; watch breakevens and term premium for slow repricing.

insights.claim: Equilibrium/neutral rates should be structurally higher given inflation persistence, making the terminal rate higher than consensus assumes.
insights.speaker: Colin Lancaster
insights.so_what: Higher r-star supports being structurally short duration and positions for steeper curves and more rates volatility.

themes.theme: Equity Vol Regime: Spot-Up-Vol-Up & Record-Low Correlation

insights.claim: The realized correlation of S&P 500 single stocks over the past month is about 3%, with no historical precedent, which mechanically dampens index volatility and the VIX.
insights.speaker: Colin Lancaster
insights.so_what: Record-low correlation artificially suppresses index vol; a correlation spike would re-rate the VIX violently, favoring long index vol / dispersion unwind hedges.
insights.quote: the the correlation of the stocks in the S&P over the last month is 3%. It's just beyond unusual

insights.claim: Memory and chip shortages (DRAM) are creating a short-squeeze-like 'spot-up-vol-up' dynamic where stocks like Micron and SK Hynix rise toward trillion-dollar caps while implied vol rises too.
insights.speaker: Colin Lancaster
insights.so_what: Spot-up-vol-up signals a crowded, squeezed condition rather than healthy melt-up; the same names are most exposed to a violent reversal.
insights.quote: A short squeeze is the ultimate VA event. There's nothing more protracted than a short squeeze. And that's kind of what you have in memory chips.

insights.claim: The largest single-stock leveraged ETF is a 3x product on SK Hynix with over $10 billion of assets and a vertical chart, illustrating how product innovation amplifies crowded directional bets.
insights.speaker: Dean Curnutt
insights.so_what: Concentrated 3x ETF assets are forced sellers into a drawdown, magnifying downside gappiness in the underlying single names.
insights.quote: the largest leveraged ETF on a single stock basis is a 3xer on SKH Heinix. It's got over 10 billion of assets and the chart is just straight vertical.

insights.claim: The largest multi-manager platforms have all existed 30-40 years and their accumulated investment in systems, data, technology and fortress balance sheets (near-permanent capital) now constitutes a widening competitive moat.
insights.speaker: Colin Lancaster
insights.so_what: Scale advantages mean capital concentrates in incumbents; new pod-shop startups will struggle to be funded, a consolidation theme for allocators.
insights.quote: I think those competitive moes will continue to get a bit wider and you know it is the larger of the firms that will be able to to house the most the more complex strategies

insights.claim: Big allocators are unlikely to fund additional multi-manager startups; a single-manager spinout can raise capital but can rarely transform itself into a true multi-manager.
insights.speaker: Colin Lancaster
insights.so_what: Reinforces a barbell where capital flows to established platforms; LP due diligence should discount new pod-shop launches.

insights.claim: Schoenfeld is a nearly 40-year-old firm founded by mathematician Steven Schoenfeld with a higher allocation to systematic/quant strategies and more internationally deployed capital (strong APAC and EMEA franchises) than peers.
insights.speaker: Colin Lancaster
insights.so_what: A firm's founder DNA dictates its dominant, most-embedded strategies; Schoenfeld's quant lineage and international tilt differentiate its return engine.

insights.claim: Schoenfeld organizes around four business verticals: systematic, fundamental long-short equity, macro fixed income, and tactical (delta-1, EM, index rebalance, event-oriented); it notably lacks a large commodities effort.
insights.speaker: Colin Lancaster
insights.so_what: Maps where Schoenfeld competes and where it is underweight (commodities), useful for understanding its factor exposures and hiring direction.

insights.claim: Macro fixed income earns a seat in the pod model precisely because it has had zero correlation to the firm's other strategies and aims to add convexity during risk-off periods when other books struggle.
insights.speaker: Colin Lancaster
insights.so_what: Explains why platforms keep allocating to macro despite lumpy returns; it functions as portfolio-level convexity, not standalone alpha.

insights.claim: A risk event is fundamentally an unwind of crowding: a crowded belief that gets shattered forces capital to move quickly, producing gappy price action and overshoots.
insights.speaker: Dean Curnutt
insights.so_what: Monitoring crowded positioning, not valuation, is the better early-warning signal for sharp drawdowns.
insights.quote: a good risk event is just an an unwind of crowding, right? It's a crowded condition. It's a crowded belief and it's capital that needs to move quickly because the belief got shattered.

insights.claim: Maintaining a 3-4% average pairwise correlation across a pod portfolio means that even in a tail event correlations top out around 11-12%, which is manageable; problems arise only when correlations move toward 1.
insights.speaker: Colin Lancaster
insights.so_what: Quantifies the threshold separating a survivable drawdown from a career-ending one; correlation drift is the core risk metric for platform heads.
insights.quote: if you can maintain an average pair-wise correlation of three or 4%... into a tail event you you can... feel comfortable that yes you'll see a pickup in correlations but that's probably going to top out at 11 12%

insights.claim: Years of overly accommodative monetary and fiscal policy have encouraged heavy leverage, and crowded levered unwinds (e.g. put-selling in front-end rates) produce regime-shift gappy moves like the March repricing in UK and Euro-zone front-end rates.
insights.speaker: Colin Lancaster
insights.so_what: Levered short-vol positioning in rates is a recurring source of unpriced regime shifts; watch front-end rate vol for crowded-unwind risk.

insights.claim: Banks no longer provide liquidity and no new liquidity providers have emerged in fixed income (unlike Jane Street/Citadel Securities in equities), so the Treasury and gilt markets break down in stress and central banks have an unspoken mandate to be liquidity provider of last resort.
insights.speaker: Colin Lancaster
insights.so_what: Structural fixed-income liquidity fragility means stress events will be sharper and the Fed/ECB backstop is effectively priced in; fade extreme spread widening expecting intervention.
insights.quote: one of the you know central banks and the feds you know unspoken mandates now is to be the liquidity provider of you when the markets need it.

insights.claim: Crowding from external firms can hurt you even when you are right: being on the other side of Brian Hunter's Amaranth nat-gas calendar spread in 2006 produced painful days as his ability to keep pushing the market signaled you were wrong on price.
insights.speaker: Colin Lancaster
insights.so_what: Understanding whose capital sits alongside you on both long and short legs is essential; mark-to-market pressure can force you out of a correct thesis.

insights.claim: A flashy two-year track record now matters less than resilient character: candidates who can articulate a specific edge, explain how they cut risk and reset during drawdowns, and are intellectually honest about their strategy's tails.
insights.speaker: Colin Lancaster
insights.so_what: Edge articulation plus tail honesty is the underwriting filter; a PM who claims to be too-good-in-every-market is a red flag.
insights.quote: I think I'm less impressed by that flashy two-year track record and much more impressed by someone who shows strong characteristics.

insights.claim: The biggest underwriting mistake a strategy head can make is being surprised by how a PM performs under a given market condition; that surprise is itself the problem to solve.
insights.speaker: Colin Lancaster
insights.so_what: Predictability of behavior across regimes, not just returns, is the underwriting goal; surprise in PM behavior corrupts portfolio-level correlation assumptions.
insights.quote: the the biggest mistake I feel I can make now is going through an underwriting exercise and then being surprised.

insights.claim: Anyone who has not trained up an AI agent for manual work (e.g. vibe coding with Claude) is already being left behind, with model use-case improvements over the prior four to six months described as astounding.
insights.speaker: Colin Lancaster
insights.so_what: AI tooling is becoming table stakes for productivity in macro/quant shops; firms not adopting agents face a competitive disadvantage.
insights.quote: anyone that has not trained up an agent or two to be doing this this more manual work is already being left behind in in my opinion.

themes.theme: Sovereign Bonds, Term Premium & Fed Chair Transition

insights.claim: The marginal duration buyer has shifted from price-insensitive central banks and liability-driven pensions/insurers (now underwater and more price-sensitive) toward buyers demanding more risk premium, injecting term premium into long-end curves.
insights.speaker: Colin Lancaster
insights.so_what: Structural loss of price-insensitive duration demand argues for steeper curves over time; long-end supply/demand is deteriorating.

insights.claim: Japan is the base case and ultimate experiment for sovereign debt; the whole developed world will undergo a gradual 'Japanification' and the UK long end will be a 'limp-along' rather than an acute tipping point because governments use yield-curve control and supply restriction to manage prices.
insights.speaker: Colin Lancaster
insights.so_what: Curves steepen structurally but governments cap acute blowups; favor steepeners over outright crisis bets, and respect official intervention around 10-year yields.
insights.quote: I think the whole world is going to go through a bit of a chapenification at at some point

insights.claim: Incoming Fed chair Kevin Warsh stands for a smaller balance sheet, less forward guidance and a much higher threshold for QE, but his most achievable lever is forward guidance, not the balance sheet, which Lorie Logan effectively controls.
insights.speaker: Colin Lancaster
insights.so_what: Expect reduced per-meeting forward guidance under Warsh (more rates vol and ELF/relative-value opportunity) but limited near-term balance-sheet change.
insights.quote: I think he has the most ability to impact the forward guidance component

insights.claim: Warsh's stated preference to let markets run their course will likely buckle in a real crisis: when the Treasury market breaks, bond-basis books blow up and politicians warn of recession and job losses, returning to the QE playbook becomes politically easy.
insights.speaker: Colin Lancaster
insights.so_what: Discount the hawkish 'let markets clear' brand in a true stress event; the QE put likely re-emerges, capping how far a sell-off is allowed to run.

themes.theme: Crude, AI Resource Demand & the Near-Term Risk

insights.claim: Crude oil is the key transmission mechanism into broader macro and inflation persistence; even with an Iran headline, the likely outcome is a 'skinny non-deal' prolonged ceasefire that keeps crude elevated, with inventory cushions delaying the inflation bite until later this year.
insights.speaker: Colin Lancaster
insights.so_what: A sustained energy shock keeps inflation above target and pressures the Fed post-midterms; watch crude as the lead indicator for the next inflation/policy juncture.
insights.quote: what end ends up happening is what I would describe as a skinny non-deal where... crude remains at more elevated prices for a while.

insights.claim: AI is disinflationary long term (depending on token pricing) but inflationary in the short term because, unlike prior tech cycles, it competes for labor, power, commodities, financing and credit-market capacity, raising equilibrium rates.
insights.speaker: Colin Lancaster
insights.so_what: Near-term AI capex is an inflation and growth story that supports higher rates, countering the consensus that AI is immediately disinflationary; favors energy/power and short-duration exposure.
insights.quote: ultimately I do think it is disinflationary depending on how the pricing of tokens plays out, but certainly in the short term I I think it is inflationary.

insights.claim: Overall conditions are supportive for risk, but the dominant tail risk is the unwind of crowded positions piled into the AI/memory theme and 3x/leveraged ETFs, where it wouldn't take much of a pullback to spiral and set markets back.
insights.speaker: Colin Lancaster
insights.so_what: The convexity is to the downside in the most crowded thematic names; size hedges around AI/memory concentration even while staying constructive on broad risk.
insights.quote: it is the unwind of these crowded positions that can quickly spiral out of control and create a lot of pain. And that is the type of risk that keeps me up at night.

insights.claim: The market is choosing to look at enormous S&P earnings rather than the potential fallout from an energy shock that shows no sign of abating, a fallout that may only be felt months down the road.
insights.speaker: Dean Curnutt
insights.so_what: Today's setup is an earnings bubble risk (not a 2000-style valuation bubble) masking a lagging energy/inflation hit; positioning should account for delayed transmission.
insights.quote: the market is just deciding to look at the earnings instead of the potential fallout from the energy shock which to me shows maybe no sign of a baiting
